extra.message: User-controlled input `name` from POST data is concatenated directly into a SQL query via Python %-formatting in the Student.create method. The value of `name` flows from `views.py:57` (`data['name']`) through `Student.create(conn, data['name'])` and is injected unsanitized into the SQL string 'VALUES (''%(name)s'')' % {'name': name}. An attacker can provide a malicious name value containing SQL metacharacters to manipulate the query, enabling full database compromise.

Jinja2 autoescaping is explicitly disabled via `autoescape=False` when setting up the template engine. This means ALL template variables are rendered without HTML escaping by default. Combined with user-controlled data stored in the database (student names, course titles/descriptions, review texts), this enables stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by submitting malicious input through any POST form, which will execute when other users view the affected pages.

`course.description` is rendered without HTML escaping (`{{ course.description }}`) and autoescape is disabled globally. The description value originates from user POST input (`data['description']` in `views.py:90`) via Course.create. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the course description field, which will execute in browsers of all users viewing that course page.

`review.review_text` is rendered without HTML escaping (`{{ review.review_text }}`) and autoescape is disabled globally. The review_text value originates from user POST input (`data.get('review_text')` in `views.py:121`) via Review.create. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into course reviews, which will execute in browsers of all users viewing that course page. This is a stored XSS vulnerability.

`course.title` (line 17) and `course.description` (line 18) are rendered without HTML escaping and autoescape is disabled globally. Both values originate from user POST input via `data['title']` and `data['description']` in `views.py:89-90` through Course.create. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into course data, executing in the browsers of all users viewing the courses listing page.

`name` is rendered without HTML escaping (`{{ name }}`) and autoescape is disabled globally. The name value originates from user POST input (`data['name']` in `views.py:57`) through Student.create which uses a SQL-injectable query. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the student name, executing in the browsers of all users viewing the students listing page.

MD5 is used for password hashing via `md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()`. MD5 is cryptographically broken and susceptible to fast brute-force attacks. Password hashes can be cracked in seconds using modern hardware. The password hash for user 'superadmin' is stored as `md5('superadmin')` in the database fixture (migrations/001-fixtures.sql:10), meaning the plaintext 'superadmin' can be immediately identified via a rainbow table or MD5 reverse lookup.

The CSRF protection middleware (`csrf_middleware`) is commented out in the application middleware stack. While CSRF tokens are generated and embedded in forms via `csrf_processor` and `csrf_token()`, they are never validated because the middleware that checks them is disabled. This allows attackers to craft cross-site request forgery attacks: a victim authenticated user can be tricked into submitting POST requests (create students, courses, reviews, evaluations, or even log out) from an external attacker-controlled site.

The session cookie is configured with `httponly=False` in the RedisStorage constructor. This means the session ID cookie is accessible to client-side JavaScript via `document.cookie`. If an attacker successfully exploits any XSS vulnerability, they can steal the session cookie and impersonate the victim user. Additionally, no `secure` or `samesite` flags are configured, making the cookie also accessible over unencrypted HTTP connections and vulnerable to CSRF via cross-site requests.

cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute

extra.message: The 50x error template dumps the entire exception object's dictionary via `{{ error.__dict__ }}`. This exposes internal application state including potentially sensitive information such as database queries, connection strings, file paths, stack traces, and other implementation details to end users. This information can aid attackers in crafting more targeted attacks against the application.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message

extra.message: Database credentials are hardcoded in plaintext in the configuration file: `user: postgres` and `password: postgres`. If this configuration is committed to version control or deployed to production, it exposes database credentials. While this file is in a development configuration, it poses a risk if accidentally deployed or if the repository is made public.
